refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safety). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified design parameter, not part of the FR itself. The FR now resides purely in the problem domain—what capability must exist—without prescribing how latency will be achieved or what specific threshold defines 'safe.'
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator command output) under agreed network conditions. Validate that measured latency supports safe operation as defined in the worksite safety plan and operational policies. Cross-reference with tele-operator acceptance testing and safety manager sign-off.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs to remote equipment actuators.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment sensor data and state information to the tele-operator interface.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain bidirectional communication continuity during teleoperation sessions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, encoding, transmission, decoding, and actuation stages, with active monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service controls to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms under specified network conditions.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel architecture (primary high-fidelity + fallback low-bandwidth) with automatic failover logic, allowing graceful degradation of teleoperation capability when primary channel latency exceeds threshold, ensuring operator safety during transient network degradation.

DP3: Decompose the teleoperation pipeline into independent functional stages (sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, actuator control) with explicit latency budgets per stage and independent optimization/monitoring of each stage to isolate sources of latency drift.

DP4: Employ a predictive state synchronization model where the remote system maintains a forward-predicted state model of the tele-operated asset, reducing operator-perceived latency through local prediction while validating against authoritative updates, with safety interlocks to prevent divergence.

DP5: Implement a hybrid synchronous-asynchronous communication model where critical safety commands use synchronous acknowledgment (ensuring delivery) while non-critical telemetry uses asynchronous streaming, optimizing latency for safety-critical paths.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose the teleoperation pipeline into independent functional stages (sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, actuator control) with explicit latency budgets per stage and independent optimization/monitoring of each stage to isolate sources of latency drift.

potential_sources: Coupling between encoding strategy and network transport protocol (e.g., compression ratio vs. bandwidth availability); mitigated by treating encoding as a separate stage with defined input/output contracts.
Coupling between command decoding and actuator control timing; mitigated by explicit handoff interface and independent latency budgets.
Coupling between sensor acquisition rate and state encoding frequency; mitigated by buffering and rate-decoupling mechanisms at stage boundaries.
Implicit coupling to network conditions (unavoidable); managed through explicit monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service controls within the transport stage, not diffused across other stages.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves high modularity by enforcing stage independence through well-defined interfaces and latency contracts. Each stage can be optimized, tested, and monitored independently. Coupling is localized to stage boundaries and network conditions, which is inherent to the problem domain. Alternative DPs (DP1, DP2, DP4, DP5) introduce tighter coupling: DP1 is monolithic; DP2 adds complexity through dual-channel logic; DP4 couples operator perception to prediction accuracy; DP5 couples safety semantics to communication model.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are stable and predictable within the agreed operational envelope (latency, jitter, packet loss). RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond assumptions, p95 ≤ 150 ms may not be achievable; mitigation requires fallback policies (DP2 hybrid approach) or operational constraints.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor acquisition, encoding, decoding, and actuation hardware/firmware can be tuned to meet individual stage latency budgets. RISK: Legacy or off-the-shelf components may have fixed latencies that exceed allocated budgets; mitigation requires early hardware selection and latency profiling.

ASSUMPTION: Operator can tolerate p95 latency of 150 ms for safe remote operation under the worksite safety plan. RISK: If actual operational tasks require lower latency (e.g., precision manipulation), the FR may be unachievable; mitigation requires validation with operational stakeholders.

ASSUMPTION: Monitoring and adaptive controls can detect and respond to latency drift within the 150 ms budget window. RISK: Detection/response latency itself consumes budget; mitigation requires pre-computed control policies and hardware-level monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Stage boundaries can be cleanly defined and interfaces can be standardized without introducing additional latency overhead. RISK: Interface marshaling, serialization, or synchronization may add latency; mitigation requires careful interface design and benchmarking.

ASSUMPTION: The worksite safety plan includes operational policies that account for 150 ms latency (e.g., reduced speed, geofencing, operator training). RISK: If safety plan assumes lower latency, the FR and DP may be misaligned; mitigation requires explicit co-design with safety stakeholders.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator, achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions, with explicit latency budgets allocated to sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, and actuator control stages. The solution shall include monitoring and adaptive controls to detect and respond to latency drift, and shall integrate with the worksite safety plan to ensure operator safety during transient network degradation or latency excursions. Success criteria: (1) p95 latency ≤ 150 ms measured end-to-end under nominal network conditions; (2) graceful degradation or failover when latency exceeds threshold; (3) operator training and safety procedures validated for 150 ms latency; (4) latency monitoring and alerting integrated into operator interface.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the complete teleoperation pipeline including sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, and actuator control stages, with explicit latency budgeting and per-stage monitoring infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports sensor-acquired state and encoded telemetry data to external consumers; carries latency metadata per pipeline stage.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level operator commands and decoded actuation directives; includes latency budget constraints and priority signals.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network transport interface; carries encoded state and commands with explicit latency tracking per transmission.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Exports latency drift warnings and pipeline stage degradation alerts to Safety Services for intervention.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: all five pipeline stages (sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, actuator control) are logically owned by Teleoperation. Latency budgeting and per-stage monitoring are internal concerns. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services, but this is a clean boundary.

Poor containment: Vehicle owns actuator control but not sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, or command decoding. The DP spans multiple vehicle subsystems and external teleoperation logic; high diffusion across Vehicle and Teleoperation boundaries.

Very poor containment: Safety Services is a consumer of latency alerts, not the owner of the pipeline. The DP is fundamentally about pipeline decomposition and latency isolation, which is orthogonal to safety policy enforcement. Massive diffusion.

Moderate containment: NGHE Solution is a system-level container that could logically own Teleoperation as a subsystem. However, this is overly broad; the DP is specific to the teleoperation pipeline, not the entire NGHE solution. Diffusion occurs because NGHE also owns Vehicle and Safety Services, which are not part of the pipeline decomposition.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP3. It naturally owns all five pipeline stages and the latency budgeting/monitoring logic. The DP decomposes a functional pipeline into independent stages with explicit latency tracking—this is intrinsically a teleoperation concern. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are clean and well-defined, with minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: Latency monitoring and per-stage optimization may require deep integration with Vehicle actuator control and sensor hardware; if Vehicle subsystems do not expose sufficient latency observability, the DP may be difficult to realize without Vehicle module changes.

Network Link interface abstraction must be sufficiently detailed to allow Teleoperation to track and budget latency per transmission; if Network Link hides transport-layer latency, the DP's latency isolation goal is compromised.

SafetyAlert interface assumes Safety Services can act on latency drift warnings; if Safety Services lacks authority to intervene in the pipeline (e.g., cannot throttle commands or trigger fallback modes), the DP's monitoring value is reduced.

Sensor acquisition stage ownership assumes Teleoperation can directly interface with Vehicle sensors; if sensors are tightly coupled to Vehicle control logic, decoupling may require Vehicle module refactoring.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The Teleoperation IM boundary is well-scoped and provides explicit latency budgeting per stage (sensor acquisition, state encoding, network transport, command decoding, actuator control). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and traceable to per-stage latency budgets. Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces export latency metadata, enabling independent verification of each stage's contribution to end-to-end latency. However, verification depends critically on Vehicle and Network Link modules exposing sufficient latency observability (see containment risks).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: GOOD. The FR requirement (safe remote operation via teleoperation with bounded latency) is clearly allocated to the Teleoperation IM. The five pipeline stages are explicitly named and owned. Latency budgeting is an internal concern of Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) define clean handoff points. Ambiguity exists only at the Vehicle boundary: sensor acquisition and actuator control stages require Vehicle subsystem cooperation, but ownership and interface contracts are not fully detailed.

diffusion_risks: SENSOR ACQUISITION STAGE: Teleoperation claims ownership of sensor acquisition, but sensors are typically owned and controlled by Vehicle. If Vehicle sensors lack latency observability or are tightly coupled to Vehicle control logic, Teleoperation cannot independently optimize or monitor this stage without Vehicle refactoring. Risk: DP latency isolation goal is compromised if sensor latency is opaque.

ACTUATOR CONTROL STAGE: Teleoperation owns command decoding but Vehicle owns actuator control. The boundary between 'decoded command' and 'actuator actuation' must be precisely defined with explicit latency tracking. If Vehicle actuator control introduces unbudgeted latency (e.g., internal command queuing, safety interlocks), the DP's per-stage latency isolation fails. Risk: Latency drift in Vehicle actuator subsystem is not visible to Teleoperation monitoring.

NETWORK LINK ABSTRACTION: The Network Link interface must expose per-transmission latency (encoding, transmission, decoding delays). If Network Link hides transport-layer latency behind a simple send/receive API, Teleoperation cannot decompose network latency into constituent parts. Risk: DP's latency isolation goal is undermined; network latency becomes a black box.

SAFETY ALERT FEEDBACK LOOP: SafetyAlert interface exports latency drift warnings, but Safety Services is a consumer, not a co-owner. If Safety Services lacks authority to intervene in the pipeline (e.g., cannot throttle Tasking commands, trigger fallback modes, or pause sensor acquisition), the monitoring value is reduced to passive alerting. Risk: DP assumes Safety Services can act on alerts; if not, latency drift is detected but not mitigated.

VEHICLE-TELEOPERATION COUPLING: The DP spans two IM boundaries (Teleoperation and Vehicle). Sensor acquisition and actuator control stages require Vehicle subsystem changes to expose latency observability. If Vehicle module resists or delays these changes, the DP cannot be fully realized. Risk: High diffusion across Teleoperation and Vehicle boundaries; DP realization depends on Vehicle module cooperation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation IM achieves 95% containment for the core pipeline decomposition logic (latency budgeting, per-stage monitoring, state encoding, network transport). The 5% diffusion is acceptable and well-managed via clean external interfaces. However, the DP's realization (IM layer) depends on Vehicle and Network Link modules exposing latency observability. Recommend explicit interface contracts: (1) Vehicle sensors must report acquisition latency; (2) Vehicle actuators must report actuation latency; (3) Network Link must expose per-transmission latency; (4) Safety Services must define intervention authority (throttling, fallback modes). Without these contracts, the DP is logically sound but practically difficult to realize.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR requirement. The FR demands 'latency that permits safe remote operation' (acceptance criterion: p95 ≤ 150 ms). DP3 decomposes the teleoperation pipeline into independent stages with explicit latency budgets per stage and per-stage monitoring. This decomposition enables: (1) identification of latency bottlenecks (which stage exceeds budget?), (2) independent optimization of each stage, (3) early detection of latency drift via monitoring, (4) safety intervention via SafetyAlert interface. The DP is necessary and sufficient to achieve the FR under the stated acceptance criteria. No logical gaps detected.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 - VEHICLE INTERFACE CONTRACT: Explicitly define latency observability requirements for Vehicle module. Specify that Vehicle sensors must expose 'acquisition_latency_ms' metadata and Vehicle actuators must expose 'actuation_latency_ms' metadata. Add acceptance criterion: Vehicle sensor latency ≤ 30 ms (p95), Vehicle actuator latency ≤ 20 ms (p95). This ensures Teleoperation can monitor and budget sensor and actuator stages independently.

REVISION 2 - NETWORK LINK INTERFACE DETAIL: Enhance Network Link interface specification to include per-transmission latency breakdown: (a) encoding latency (Teleoperation -> Network), (b) transmission latency (Network), (c) decoding latency (Network -> Teleoperation). Add acceptance criterion: Network transport latency ≤ 80 ms (p95) under agreed network conditions. This prevents network latency from becoming a black box.

REVISION 3 - SAFETY SERVICES INTERVENTION AUTHORITY: Clarify SafetyAlert interface semantics. Define what actions Safety Services can take upon receiving latency drift warnings: (a) throttle Tasking command rate, (b) trigger fallback to lower-fidelity teleoperation mode, (c) pause sensor acquisition, (d) initiate safe shutdown. Add acceptance criterion: Safety Services must respond to latency drift alerts within 50 ms. This ensures monitoring is coupled to actionable mitigation.

REVISION 4 - LATENCY BUDGET ALLOCATION: Explicitly allocate the 150 ms p95 budget across stages: Sensor acquisition ≤ 30 ms, State encoding ≤ 15 ms, Network transport ≤ 80 ms, Command decoding ≤ 15 ms, Actuator control ≤ 20 ms (total: 160 ms; 10 ms margin for jitter). Document rationale for each allocation. This makes per-stage acceptance criteria measurable and traceable.

REVISION 5 - MONITORING INFRASTRUCTURE OWNERSHIP: Clarify whether Teleoperation owns the latency monitoring infrastructure (timers, latency aggregators, alert generators) or whether this is a shared concern with Safety Services. Recommend: Teleoperation owns per-stage latency measurement and buffering; Safety Services owns policy-driven alert generation and intervention. This reduces diffusion and clarifies ownership.

REVISION 6 - FALLBACK AND DEGRADATION MODES: Extend DP3 to specify what happens when a stage exceeds its latency budget. Define fallback modes (e.g., reduce sensor resolution, increase command batching, switch to lower-bandwidth encoding). Add acceptance criterion: System must detect and respond to latency budget violation within 100 ms. This makes the DP actionable, not just observable.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, confirming logical soundness and acceptable containment. However, the gate results expose six critical diffusion risks and interface contract gaps that must be resolved before decomposing to the next level. The safety-critical nature of teleoperation (high criticality factor) demands that these risks be addressed at the current level to prevent cascading ambiguity and verification failures downstream. Specifically: (1) Vehicle-Teleoperation coupling requires explicit latency observability contracts; (2) Network Link abstraction must expose per-transmission latency breakdown; (3) Safety Services intervention authority must be defined; (4) Latency budget allocation must be traceable to acceptance criteria; (5) Monitoring infrastructure ownership must be clarified; (6) Fallback modes must be specified. These revisions strengthen the DP and IM definitions, reducing diffusion risk and enabling clean sub-FR decomposition in the next iteration.

revise.why: The DP3 (Teleoperation Pipeline with Per-Stage Latency Budgeting and Monitoring) is logically sound but lacks sufficient detail to guide implementation and verification. The six recommended revisions must be incorporated into the DP specification to: (1) define explicit interface contracts with Vehicle and Network Link modules (REVISION 1, 2); (2) clarify Safety Services intervention semantics (REVISION 3); (3) allocate the 150 ms p95 budget across stages with rationale (REVISION 4); (4) clarify monitoring infrastructure ownership (REVISION 5); (5) specify fallback and degradation modes (REVISION 6). These revisions do not change the DP's core logic but make it sufficiently detailed and traceable to support next-level decomposition and verification. The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) should be updated concurrently to reflect these interface contracts and ownership clarifications.

assumptions: Vehicle module will expose sensor acquisition latency and actuator actuation latency via explicit metadata fields in sensor and command interfaces.
Network Link module can decompose transport latency into encoding, transmission, and decoding components and expose these via interface metadata.
Safety Services has authority to intervene in the teleoperation pipeline (throttle commands, trigger fallback modes, pause acquisition) and can respond to latency drift alerts within 50 ms.
Latency budget allocation (30 ms sensor, 15 ms encoding, 80 ms network, 15 ms decoding, 20 ms actuator) is feasible under nominal operating conditions and agreed network conditions.
Monitoring infrastructure (latency timers, aggregators, alert generators) can be owned and operated by Teleoperation without requiring Vehicle or Network Link refactoring.
Fallback modes (reduced sensor resolution, command batching, lower-bandwidth encoding) are technically feasible and do not compromise safety or mission objectives.
open_risks: VEHICLE LATENCY OBSERVABILITY: Vehicle module may lack built-in latency measurement or may resist exposing sensor/actuator latency due to architectural constraints. Mitigation: Conduct Vehicle module design review; define latency observability as a non-negotiable interface requirement; allocate Vehicle team resources for instrumentation.

NETWORK LINK ABSTRACTION LEAKAGE: Network Link module may hide transport-layer latency behind a simple send/receive API, preventing Teleoperation from decomposing network latency. Mitigation: Establish explicit interface contract with Network Link; require per-transmission latency metadata; conduct network layer design review.

SAFETY SERVICES INTERVENTION LATENCY: Safety Services may not be able to respond to latency drift alerts within 50 ms, reducing the effectiveness of the monitoring DP. Mitigation: Define Safety Services response time as an acceptance criterion; conduct Safety Services architecture review; allocate resources for alert handling infrastructure.

LATENCY BUDGET FEASIBILITY: The allocated per-stage budgets (30 ms sensor, 80 ms network, 20 ms actuator) may be infeasible under worst-case conditions or with legacy Vehicle subsystems. Mitigation: Conduct latency profiling of Vehicle and Network Link under representative operating conditions; adjust budget allocation based on empirical data; define fallback modes for budget violations.

MONITORING INFRASTRUCTURE OVERHEAD: Latency measurement and monitoring may introduce additional latency (instrumentation overhead), reducing the effective budget available for functional operations. Mitigation: Quantify monitoring overhead; allocate separate budget for instrumentation; use low-latency monitoring techniques (e.g., lock-free queues, asynchronous logging).

FALLBACK MODE SAFETY: Fallback modes (reduced sensor resolution, command batching) may introduce new safety risks (e.g., reduced situational awareness, delayed command execution). Mitigation: Conduct safety analysis of fallback modes; define fallback mode activation criteria; ensure fallback modes do not violate safety invariants.
evidence_needed: Vehicle module latency profile: Measure sensor acquisition latency (p50, p95, p99) and actuator actuation latency under representative operating conditions. Confirm feasibility of 30 ms sensor and 20 ms actuator budgets.

Network Link latency breakdown: Decompose network transport latency into encoding, transmission, and decoding components. Confirm feasibility of 80 ms network budget under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Safety Services response time: Measure Safety Services alert handling latency (from alert generation to intervention action). Confirm feasibility of 50 ms response time requirement.
Latency budget allocation rationale: Document the basis for per-stage budget allocation (30-30-15-80-15-20 ms). Justify allocation against Vehicle and Network Link constraints.
Monitoring infrastructure design: Specify latency measurement points, aggregation logic, and alert generation policy. Quantify instrumentation overhead and confirm it does not exceed 10 ms.

Fallback mode specifications: Define fallback modes (reduced resolution, command batching, lower-bandwidth encoding) with acceptance criteria. Conduct safety analysis to confirm fallback modes do not introduce new hazards.

Interface contract specifications: Document explicit interface contracts for Vehicle sensors (acquisition_latency_ms metadata), Vehicle actuators (actuation_latency_ms metadata), Network Link (per-transmission latency breakdown), and Safety Services (intervention authority and response time).

End-to-end latency validation: Conduct integrated testing of the teleoperation pipeline under representative operating conditions. Measure end-to-end latency (p50, p95, p99) and confirm compliance with 150 ms p95 acceptance criterion.
